gi_source.title: Crabmeat Omelette — Carbohydrate Content Confirmed Zero; GI Not Applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010752) reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g per 100 g for this dish. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; no starch or sugar is present in the standard preparation. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition (GL = GI x carbohydrate_g per serving / 100). University of Sydney GI Database was consulted; no direct entry for crabmeat omelette exists, and GI testing is not meaningful for foods with zero available carbohydrate. GI is set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 395 mg/100 g with zero dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 395 mg/100 g approaches the upper boundary of the moderate-concern range; patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal, and pair the dish with high-potassium vegetables to leverage the 259 mg/100 g potassium content for partial blood-pressure offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; very high protein 17.88 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 0.0 g carbohydrate and 0.0 g sugar per 100 g, crabmeat omelette has no direct glycaemic impact and is well-suited for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; however, patients should avoid sweet chilli sauce accompaniments and monitor total sodium intake given the cardiovascular co-morbidity risk associated with diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 97 mg/100 g and sodium 395 mg/100 g; saturated fat 3.2 g/100 g (supplementary value)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cholesterol at 97 mg/100 g and sodium at 395 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; patients with established CVD should limit portion size to approximately 150 g per meal, choose reduced-sodium soy sauce, and note that the low total fat (0.74 g/100 g per HPB) is a partially mitigating factor, though the supplementary saturated fat figure of 3.2 g/100 g warrants monitoring at larger portion sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crabmeat Omelette (Chinese: 螃蟹蛋饼; Malay: Telur Dadar Ketam) is a popular Singaporean egg-based dish found at hawker centres, coffeeshops, and Chinese-Malay casual restaurants across the island. It is prepared by beating eggs and folding in seasoned crab meat — typically blue swimmer crab, mud crab, or flower crab — along with finely sliced spring onions, light soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil, then pan-frying the mixture in a lightly oiled wok or flat pan until the exterior is golden and slightly crisp while the interior remains soft and custardy. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with the natural sweetness of crab meat complementing the eggy base. Nutritionally, the dish is exceptionally high in protein at 17.88 g per 100 g and very low in carbohydrate at 0.0 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is 395 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is 97 mg per 100 g from the egg and crab components. Calcium (91 mg/100 g), potassium (259 mg/100 g), and magnesium (36 mg/100 g) are notable micronutrients. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 208 kcal, 44.7 g protein, and 988 mg sodium. The dish is halal-compatible when prepared without pork-derived ingredients and is dairy-free. It is commonly served as a main dish for lunch or dinner, often accompanied by steamed rice or consumed as a standalone protein dish at zi char stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a crab omelette?
Is crab omelette good for diabetics?
Can I eat crab omelette if I have high blood pressure?
Is crab omelette high in protein?
How much sodium is in a crab omelette?

Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally attributed to a non-approved secondary platform in the input record; these are retained as supplementary values with reduced confidence (0.70) and are fully documented in notes. They do not affect the primary source confidence rating.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a protein-dominant egg-and-crab dish with no starch or sugar ingredients in the standard preparation; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo canonical record. sugar_g is confirmed zero by the same source. Fat_g at 0.74 g/100 g is a genuine low value reflecting minimal added oil in pan-frying and the lean nature of crab meat. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean crabmeat omelette preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. SECONDARY SOURCE NOTE: saturated_fat_g (3.2), trans_fat_g (0.08), and vitamin_c_mg (2.0) were attributed to a non-approved delivery platform in the original input per_100g_meta. These values have been retained as nutritionally plausible supplementary figures with confidence 0.70 but are not used as primary evidence. A future revision should seek HPB or USDA FoodData Central confirmation for these three fields. Note that saturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100 g) exceeds total fat_g (0.74 g/100 g) as reported by HPB — this internal inconsistency is flagged for human review; the saturated fat value may reflect a different preparation or portion assumption in the secondary source. 4. GI: glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g per HPB Singapore Foodo). GI is set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and has no direct entry for crabmeat omelette; GI testing is not meaningful for zero-carbohydrate foods. 5. PORTIONS: the input contained two identical duplicate portion entries (1 plate, 250 g); deduplicated to a single serving entry of 250 g.
